videos.transcript: Ljubljana the capital of Slovenia and home to around 300.000 people. It's one of the smallest and most environmentally friendly cities in Europe. Hi I am Martijn your guide in Ljubljana. After watching this travel guide you know a lot more about this city to get the most out of your trip. From this famous castle... ....to a boat ride... ...beautiful churches... ...and the best Rooftop Bar. I don't like to ask but please like subscribe or leave a comment... ...that's the way YouTube Works to grow and to make new videos for you in the future. The airport is located 30 kilometers outside the city center and to travel to the city... ...you can use a bus service for example. The journey takes about 40-50 minutes and a single ticket will set you back around 5 euros. Taxis are expensive around 40 euros and when you travel by train there are a lot of direct connections to other big European cities. And from the train station it's only a 10 minutes walk to the old city center. In a moment you know more about the old town and a way to rent bikes for almost nothing. But first the "Ljubljana Castle". It was built in the 15th century on this hill. It's 376 meters high. To go to the top you have some options. Of course you can walk it's like 15 to 20 minutes and a really good exercise... ...of course by car or you can take this funicular. A return ticket will set you back around 4 euros. On top you have a beautiful view of the city of course and you can visit the castle inside. Some parts are free to go for other parts you need a ticket. And you can easily buy a combination ticket for the funicular and the castle for 13 euros. When you have that ticket you can climb to the top of this tower. Or visit the Museum of Puppetry. I have to say this Castle is around 900 years old but it looks really new when you compare it to other castles and other European cities. But it's a beautiful viewing point and a nice place to visit when you are here. Behind the castle there are some paths into the nature so when you like a nice walk it's a pretty good place to go. One of the old buildings you certainly have to visit is the Town Hall. The original building was built in Gothic style in 1484. Between 1717 and 1719 the building underwent of Baroque renovation with a Venetian inspiration. It's located on the Town Square close to the Ljubljana Cathedral and later more about that. You can visit the Town Hall completely free of charge. In a moment more about the bridges in the old Center of Ljubljana and a spot you can't miss is the "Prešeren Square". It's located in the middle of the old center. The square takes its name from Slovenia's greatest and most famous poem writer "France Prešeren". In the middle of the square you will also find a statue of him. The main building on the site is The Franciscan church it was built between 1646 and 1660... ...and it replaced an older Church on the same site. And the bell towers were built later. Many of the original frescoes were destroyed during a heavy earthquake in 1895 by cracks in the ceiling... ...and new paintings were made in 1936. You can visit the church daily for free but keep in mind that it is closed between 12:30 and 1:30 pm. The city experienced two heavy earthquakes in 1511 and 1895. The reconstruction gave the city a contemporary image with Art Nouveau masterpieces and baroque architecture. The easiest way to explore the old center is to walk around over here. It's beautiful full with colorful houses and those houses give this part of the city just so much charm and character. And when you like ice creams this is your heaven. There is an ice cream store don't ask me why but on every corner... ...and in the summer, I'm here in August, there are some street performances in the evening. The city is split into two parts by the 41 kilometer long "Ljubljanica River". You can book a boat ride for around 10 euros with different companies. There are many bridges to cross the river for example the "Butcher's Bridge" ... also called the love bridge. The "Triple Bridge" next to the "Prešeren Square" and the "Dragon Bridge". The bridge was built in 1901 and reversed to the symbol of the city. There are four stone dragons two on each side that guard the bridge. Further it's a normal Bridge with a lot of traffic. In a moment you know a great place to chill and how to rent a bike for less... ...but first the best time to visit Ljubljana. I think that will be the spring and summer. In the spring the temperature is around 20 degrees Celsius and it can be cold by night around 10 degrees Celsius. In the summer just like today in August it can be hot 25 to 30 degrees Celsius. One of the best ways to explore a city is by bike and in this city you can do it almost free of charge... ...because there is a bicycle sharing system. That means that you can use a bike one hour free of charge... ...and when you bring it back to the docking station and wait for five minutes you can use it another hour. The only fee involved is one euro for one week and three euros for the whole year. You have to pay in advance and you can do that with the link in the text below the video. In a moment you know more about the Central Market and this beautiful church... ...but first everything about street art in the neighborhood "Metelkova". Once this was a military site but nowadays the military barracks are no longer recognizable. It's fully colored and decorated with art and graffiti and this area is also known for the great parties by night. And then a place which is also a meeting point for locals the Central Market. You can buy a lot of stuff over here or sit down on the terrace for a cup of coffee. The market is open Monday till Saturday and on the lower floor you will find the fish market. In a moment you know one of the best museums to visit but first a notable landmark in the city the Cathedral of Saint Nicholas. A perfect place to admire your beautiful fresco's, artwork and sculptures. If you want to escape the hustle and bustle in the city then you go to "Tivoli Park". It's the largest park in the city. Some of the artworks and buildings are really special for example the "Tivoli Castle". This is also a perfect place for a drink. A rooftop bar with a beautiful view where you can drink a cup of coffee you know that place in a second... ...but first the museum's in town and there are a lot of them. For example the National Museum of Slovenia and a Railway Museum. It's impossible to show you them all so I decided to go go to one of the museums and that's the Museum of Modern Art. Apart from housing a permanent collection of Slovenian art it hosts temporary exhibitions presenting new artistic practices. You can visit the Museum from Tuesday till Sunday between 10 and 6 and it's closed on public holidays. And then finally one of the best rooftop bars in town it's called "Nebotičnik"... ...and that means Skyscraper. The bar itself isn't that special but the views are. And from the terrace, together with a drink, you have a beautiful view over the city and the castle. Please give a thumbs up thank you so much when you like this Ljubljana travel guide. And if you don't want to miss any other episodes just subscribe to my YouTube channel. It's totally free and it enriches your view on the whole world.

This channel documents things I have learned, cultural shocks, cultural differences,  life in Slovenia and abroad and occasional stream of thought videos. videos.transcript: Most people planning a Central Europe trip have heard of these capital cities: Prague, Vienna, Budapest. But Ljubljana? If it shows up at all, it's usually a quick stop on the way to somewhere else. We stayed 14 days. One thing we did right, we spent a day at Lake Bled, 45 minutes away, and we'll tell you how easy that is, even without a car. >> What we didn't do right, we were using the city completely wrong. And we didn't figure that out until we were almost done. We'll share our full cost at the end because Ljubljana will surprise you, just not in the direction you'd expect. >> I'm Judy. >> And I'm Kevin. Welcome back to Find Gene Marie, where we share our lives as full-time travelers and the connections we make along the way. >> If you're not familiar where Ljubljana is, Slovenia is a small country in Central Europe, roughly the size of New Jersey. And from Ljubljana, you can reach some extraordinary places within an hour. We'll come back to that. We arrived by FlixBus from Trieste, Italy, about 90 minutes away. >> It's closer to Italy than most people realize. >> And you feel that the moment you start eating here. Some of the best pizza we've eaten was from Pop's Pizza. When we arrived by bus, it was raining, so we ended up taking an Uber to our apartment. And I was surprised at how far away he dropped us off, and then realized, "Oh, this is all pedestrian only." >> And that's something we really enjoy about many of the places we travel to. Pedestrian areas are some of our favorite places because we really like to be in walkable cities, and this definitely is one of them. >> It turned out that was Congress Square, where a lot of activities are happening all through our stay there. >> It's pretty much the heart of the city. >> Now, this pedestrian-only area also invited bikes, which meant that we were kind of sharing the same spaces, and the people on bikes were going much faster than us walking around. >> And there are a lot of bikes. >> One thing we love in cities is a water feature, and Ljubljana has this beautiful river that runs through it, a lot of boats, a lot of restaurants, a lot of activities going on around it, which always is a highlight for us. >> My first impression upon being dropped off by our Uber was how cool the architecture was here. And when we were walking along the river, it was just amazing all of the beautiful buildings. And it turns out that in 1895 there was an earthquake that leveled a significant part of the city. And so all of that area had to be rebuilt, which was done in Art Nouveau and Art Deco styles with a lot of influence from Vienna. Now one of the reasons that all of the architecture looks so unified is because there was one architect whose hands were on so much of the new construction. His name was Jože Plečnik. We'll be talking about him a bit. >> One thing that you're going to see in Ljubljana is dragons. There's a dragon bridge, there's dragon souvenirs, there's dragons everywhere, even lamp posts and dragon facades. It's just the symbol of the city. >> And when they were rebuilding the city, Ljubljana went all in on this dragon theme. A couple of the things that we did wrong was not go on an architectural tour of the city and also not visit Jože Plečnik's house. His effect on Ljubljana has been compared to what Gaudí did to Barcelona. >> And once you know his name, you're going to see his influence everywhere. >> In fact, he even worked on the renovation of the Prague Castle. There are several bridges in Ljubljana, but the Triple Bridge is probably one you'll photograph on your first day there. And of course, it was Plečnik. The covered market colonnades are Plečnik. The classical stone doorway built directly into a 2,000-year-old Roman wall, Plečnik. And he even redesigned the area of town that we were in, Congress Square, which is now UNESCO listed as part of his legacy. One of the things that we really enjoyed was seeing all of the little bronze sculptures that were everywhere. And a lot of them were from Jakov Brdar. In fact, he spent decades placing his bronze figures throughout the city center. On the Butcher's Bridge, the Love Lock Bridge, you'll find his large figures, too. >> Statues like Prometheus and Adam and Eve, a satire. These are prominent statues we saw every day when we were walking down the river. >> Now, one of the ones that I particularly liked were the ducks with some pieces of bread beside them. One of the places that we missed, and I wish we hadn't, was Locksmith Street. It's a narrow medieval alleyway in the Old Town that we didn't find while we were there. There are 700 small bronze faces embedded in the gully of the lane leading to a skeleton trapped cage. They're all bird art. Now that we know this, we'll pass this along to you. Just look for the key emblem near the entrance. It's easy to walk straight past it. >> One thing I didn't expect to be searching for in Ljubljana was a mlekomat, which is basically a milk machine. >> So, basically, a local dairy farmer owns and operates the machine, and every day they change the milk fresh from their cows that morning. You bring your own bottle, or you can buy one from the machine for about a euro. Put it under the spout, insert your coins, and it fills with fresh milk. >> And a couple was watching us fill our bottle of milk, and they just joined in right after us, and we helped them with a couple of the steps that we figured out, and talked to them actually a few minutes later, and they were thrilled with it. They actually filled the second bottle. They were so happy with the milk. >> How good is it? >> It's good and cold, and it's rich. Tastes good. Yes. Yes. 30, 40 years have I has it been since I've just drank milk? >> Yeah, we just don't do that anymore. >> Oh, but it's rich. It says skimmed, but I feel like it tastes richer than skim does. >> Well, if the last time we drank milk was a long time ago, I guess we don't know what it tastes like anymore. >> I would say that Ljubljana is a city of contrast, because in some ways, after being around a little bit, it felt kind of touristy. But then, obviously, something like this is very non-touristy. This is something that locals are used to doing. These are scattered throughout the city, so it's really not geared towards tourists. >> Yeah, we'll talk about this a little bit, but it really was a blended city. >> One small perk of staying in a place longer is that when bad weather gets in the way, it doesn't mean that you are totally missing fun experiences that you want to have. That's especially true regarding the Friday market. The first Friday that we were there, it was pouring rain and they ended up canceling it. It only runs from March till October, but it's definitely something that you want to participate in. >> And again, this is not tourist only. Locals love this market. You can see that they're coming there to get their foods and there's a lot of different foods here. Traditional Slovenian, international, local wine, a lot of different Asian foods. >> How this works is local restaurants set up in this open kitchen market and offer street versions of their restaurant foods. I think it's a great way for you to sample a restaurant you might not otherwise have gone to in an affordable way. And some people have actually said they prefer the street stall versions than even going to the restaurants themselves. >> This is way bigger than I wanted, but I was really in the mood for some vegetables and bibimbap really delivers on all of it. >> You got a little spice in there they said, right? >> Yes. Really good. Crunchy, delicious. Fresh tasting. >> One thing we found here that I was really enjoying, Slovenian orange wine. It's white wine, but it's made with extended contact to the skin of the grape. So, it turns an orange color. A lot of restaurants didn't offer orange wine. They'd say they didn't have a bottle of it, but at this market there was a special booth just for it. >> Fortunately, if you stop in a wine shop, you can probably get some version of an orange wine. >> The first day we were walking through Congress Square, they were setting up this big stage and it seemed like every day there was a different activity, a different festival, a different announcement going on about something, competitions, just so much happening every single day. >> Some of the festivals we weren't particularly interested in, but the one we really did like was the Asian Festival. And that's where it was a weekend full of Asian food. There were a whole bunch of other activities, but basically we enjoyed eating stall after stall of Asian food and it was delicious. >> At the same spot as the Central Kitchen. >> Although there's plenty to do in the heart of the city, if you step outside of that a little bit, there's also some Roman ruins that are definitely worth stopping and taking a look at. >> As you walk through the highlighted spots in this Roman Trail, you're not going to see a huge ancient building. So, don't be expecting that. There's good information on the signage as you stop and look at these different stations and there's a lot of history here. >> In fact, there's remains of a Roman house behind a fence in a residential neighborhood and we couldn't go in because it was closed by the time we arrived, but we could still take some pictures and >> I have ways of sneaking the camera through the fence. >> [laughter] >> And there is a Roman wall and one thing that you'll see which confused me a little bit cuz it's not actually Roman per se, is Plečnik built a pyramid out of the Roman stones and it's known as his pyramid. Basically a stone doorway set into the Roman wall. >> One of our favorite topics about any place is its food. >> One of the places you definitely want to visit is Slovenska Hisa Figovec. Its history dates back to 1776. I got some really delicious stuffed peppers with mashed potatoes when we were there, but there's another location that we also liked and we preferred the desserts that were there. >> [laughter] >> Which is a delicious apple and shredded pancake dessert, but it's also very big. I ate it as kind of a meal. >> [laughter] >> I opted for a traditional Slovenian dessert, which basically had poppy seeds, walnuts, cottage cheese, apple filling all inside a flaky pastry, but I do have to admit that I preferred Kevin's dish over mine. >> Of course, we have to find a coffee shop in every city that we go and Kavarna was a wonderful spot. Now, if you're looking for a place to bring your laptop in and sit down with some Wi-Fi, this is not the place. But, if you like free trade coffee beans and a lot of detailed information about what the best bean is for the type of coffee that you want, an ideal place. They have an upstairs, which was really nice. We just walk up there and table service, and of course, the coffee was wonderful. >> And so were the croissants. They were very fresh tasting. We loved them. One of the interesting things that the barista had told us was that 3 years ago it would be 70% Slovenians and 30% tourists, and now that number has flipped. >> And they don't seem upset about it. >> It felt like there were a lot of locals because people were bringing their pets and parking their bikes. So, it definitely had a local feel to it. >> And this wasn't the only place we had fresh croissants. This town loves its baked goods. >> One coffee shop that we ate at a few times had the freshest croissants, and one time had some cookies alongside the coffee that we couldn't get enough of. And they told us that they get their baked goods from Le Gâteau Bakery. And I will tell you, it brought back so much nostalgia. It reminded me of Mexican wedding cookies just without the nuts. They were delicious. This is not a touristy bakery at all, and it's absolutely worth a 15-minute walk to get there. All you have to do practically when you're wandering through the city is look up, and you'll see Ljubljana Castle. >> The nice part is you don't have to walk up to this castle. You can take a funicular. We found the exhibits that were in this castle interesting, but they certainly weren't mind-blowing. >> This is the smallest armory museum I've ever been in. >> [laughter] >> It's more like an armory closet. >> [laughter] >> If you're wondering whether you have time for it, you do. >> [laughter] >> And it's kind of a coin flip if you actually need to go there or not. Everyone told us that we need to go to Lake Bled. It's 45 minutes away from Ljubljana, but we don't rent cars or rent a place. Looking at the options, there was a train, which everyone said, "Don't do the train. It's not really near the lake where you want to be." But, the bus was really easy and inexpensive to get there. If you don't buy your tickets online, you're going to need cash at the bus station. >> When we looked at pictures online, it would look stunning. But, of course, you don't necessarily expect it to look that good in person. It really did look that beautiful. >> [music] [music] >> We arrived at Lake Bled. We were pretty hungry. So, instead of climbing the castle stairs first, >> [music] >> we decided to stop at this Osteria with very traditional Slovenian food. >> In fact, Kevin's sausage was really enjoyable, wasn't it? >> And they're very generous portions, so we were quite stuffed when we left there. If you want to get the best stunning views of the lake, go up to Bled Castle. It's a bit of a hike. It's a switchback walkway that takes you up to the castle, and it's worth it once you get up there. >> But, there are a lot of steps. So, if you don't have good mobility, this is probably not the hike for you. It really was only 20 minutes from base to top. >> And there's also a road to get you there, so you may be able to get just an Uber ride up to the castle, so you don't have to do the big walk. >> We enjoyed this castle far more than the one in Ljubljana. So, if you only were going to see one, this is the one we think you'll enjoy more. >> The ticket price isn't cheap at this castle, but there is a lot to see, and there are also places you can sit down and enjoy the view, have a coffee, or a small snack. So, there's more up there to kind of hang out and and be for half a day. >> In fact, I think one of the must-dos, if you like dessert at all, is to get the Bled cream cake. There's a more traditional version, but this one was great, and we also got this bonus view. >> So, I think this is like a double-edged sword where it's kind of hard to break the top. So, be very careful, or you'll you'll be wearing all that powdered sugar. >> Don't sneeze. I I think I can get through it. There we go. All right, three layers. Got all three. >> Um does it remind you of a strudel? >> No. Well, maybe. Maybe. I know this whole thing reminds me of something like I've had it before. Just not this thick, maybe. >> We spent about 5 hours in Bled, and I think we enjoyed every minute of it. We did a lot of walking. The circumference of the lake is beautiful, and it was worth our time. One thing we didn't do though was take a boat to the castle that is on this little island, and maybe that would have been nice, but it was fine that we missed it. >> There's nice park areas there that you can just hang out in. Of course, you may get friendly ducks that walk by. >> After this day trip to Bled, we realized that we were looking at Ljubljana all wrong. The biggest mistake when we were here was treating Ljubljana as a destination. I think 2 weeks actually was a little bit too long, but it's so easy to get around and see so many other things that we ended up missing. >> And it's really easy to get seduced by the central area of the city. >> As we mentioned earlier, we arrived from Trieste, Italy, and that could have been a few days that you could come and go very easily from Ljubljana. There also is Postojna Cave, which people rave about, and if you're not too tired of castles, there's also Predjama Castle, and so much more. We think that Ljubljana has the potential to be one of the best bases in Europe because there are so many things nearby. >> And if you love scenic views, Slovenia is a wonderful country. >> [music] >> Let's talk about what it cost for our time in Ljubljana. >> We spent 14 days here, and we traveled by FlixBus for a mere $46 or $23 per person. Accommodations in the heart of the city, in Congress Square, were $2,291 or $164 per day. That, I think, was part of our mistake. It is very expensive to stay here, and we realized that when we were actually booking accommodations, we were planning to stay even longer, but at that price, it was a little bit out of our budget to extend our time there. Getting around the city cost $60. Dining and groceries were a total of $1,124, or $40 per person per day, which wasn't bad at all. Activities cost us a total of $120. And the total cost of our stay here was $3,641. Minus the cost of transportation to travel from Trieste, we spent $3,595, which breaks down to $257 per day, and $129 per person per day. We think it's a great hub, but it definitely didn't need a full 14 days here. So, what were our final impressions about Ljubljana? >> I liked it a lot, and I thought the river was a great influence. Certainly loved to walk up and down that. But thinking back, I don't know if there was that much to do in the city itself. >> And we are people who don't mind slow travel. So, the fact that I was ready to leave when we did, I mean, I definitely felt like there were things that I wish we'd done while we were there, but they were day trips for the most part. >> Right. I mean, if you just enjoy hanging out, having a nap or TV, or nice meals by a river, fantastic place to do that. In fact, we had one couple that said they love just to hang out on the river here. This is one of their favorite places. I just don't know if it has the depth to go the distance if you want to be in a place for a longer period of time. >> Now, we really do like pedestrian-focused cities, and we give this a five out of five in terms of mobility scale. >> Overall, I love the city. I I definitely would rate as a place you need to go. And as Judy said before, it's just not a place you may want to extend your stay, especially with the lodging costs. >> If you are looking for a long-stay destination, we highly recommend Trieste. We have a full video about it, and you can watch it here next. Thank you so much for joining us here in Slovenia. Our next stop is Riga, Latvia, and you're not going to want to miss it. If you haven't subscribed, we'd love for you to do that. >> Remember to >> Wander more. >> Worry less. >> And bring a passport. >> Until next time. >> Until next time.

videos.transcript: [music] Lubiana is one of those cities that surprises you in the best way. It's small, gorgeous, and has this perfect mix of alpine views and laid-back Mediterranean vibes. We've got two days here, so I'm taking you from the dragon bridges and cozy oldtown streets to Tavi Park for forest walks and a little history. [music] And stay till the end because I'll share my quick cheat sheet, how to get around, where to stay, and what to eat without wasting time. All right, let's go. Day one, Oldtown Core and Castle. Morning, Ljubljana Castle. Head straight to Ljubljana Castle to kick off the day. Getting here early is the move. You'll beat the loud tour groups and catch that sharp morning light hitting the Alps. This fortress was built in the 1400s as a massive power move by the Hubsburgs, but it's seen much grittier days than your average royal seat. It served as a brutal state prison and even a social housing for the poor before the city finally restored it. Don't feel pressured to hike through every single museum inside. Walk the ramparts and check out the old prison cells to see the darker side of the building's history, which covers everything from Roman times to the collapse of Yugoslavia. If the steep hike up the hill feels like too much for a morning, take the finicular and save your legs for the miles of walking ahead. A standard castle ticket costs about $16. But if you want the finicular ride included, it'll be roughly $20. If you're only here for the panorama, a return trip on the finicular alone costs about $65. Insider tip: Skip the full ticket if you're on a budget. You can actually walk into the main castle courtyard for free. Head straight for the viewing ramp part. It'll give you that massive city perspective for zero cost, leaving you more in your pocket for a glass of local wine at the castle cafe. Late morning brunch at the castle. Stay put and grab brunch at Gastela Nagradu. After conquering the ramp parts, this is the smartest move you can make. Zero backtracking and no hangry wandering. The restaurant sits right in the courtyard, serving a massive highlight reel of Slovenia's 24 culinary regions in a relaxed open air setting. Order the Cranesa Cluba, the legendary local smoked sausage, or the Idridgeki Zlick Rafi and folded dumplings that'll ruin standard pasta for you forever. Pair them with a glass of local white wine, Slovenia's vineyards produce bottles that rival the best in Europe for a fraction of the price. A solid meal here with a drink will cost between $25 and $45. You're paying for a slight premium for the location, but the quality and convenience make it worth every cent. Once you've refueled, you'll head downhill to trade the castle views for the pulse of the city streets. Afternoon Old Town and the Dragon Bridge. Once you finished at the castle, wander back down into the old town and trade the medieval fortress vibes for Ljubljana's famous riverside energy. The entire centers a car-free zone, making it one of the most walkable capitals in Europe. You won't need a map or a rigid plan here. The city is designed for drifting through narrow cobblestone alleys and popping into small artisan workshops. Follow the Emerald Green River until you hit the Dragon Bridge, the city's most iconic landmark. You'll see four massive copper green dragons guarding the corners. They look incredibly fierce and serve as the official protectors of Ljubana. Local legend says these dragons wag their tails whenever a virgin crosses the bridge, but don't hold your breath. They haven't moved in centuries. It's a quick essential stop for that I made it to Slovenia photo. The bridge is free to cross and puts you right at the edge of the central market where you can see the city's daily life in full swing. Everything in this part of town is tightly packed, so you can easily move from one bridge to the next without ever having to double back. Lubana Central Market. Just past the Dragon Bridge, you land straight in the Ljubljana Central Market. This is where the city stops performing and starts functioning. Locals come here to actually shop, not browse. And that's what makes it worth your time. The market stretches along the river and under the stone arcades with stalls packed with mountain cheeses, cured meats, local honey, and seasonal fruit. It's lively without being overwhelming, and you don't need a plan. Walk slowly, sample the pumpkin seed oil, and watch how the rhythm changes compared to the postcard spots you just came from. This is a short stop. 15 to 30 minutes is enough, but it grounds the Oldtown experience and shows how compact and livable Ljubljana really is. It's the perfect place to grab a quick snack from the bakery stall before the day moves forward. Precedent Square and Triple Bridge. From the Oldtown alleys, everything naturally funnels toward Preceden Square, the city's main heartbeat. This circular plaza is anchored by the Franciscan Church of the Enunciation, a massive salmon pink landmark you can't miss. It's the best spot to get your bearings. Stand by the statue in the center to see the entire city branching out in every direction. Step straight from the square onto the Triple Bridge. This is an architectural oddity you won't find anywhere else. Three separate pedestrian walkways fanning out across the river. The famous architect Yoja Pletchnik added the two side wings in the 1930s to fix traffic bottlenecks, but today the entire Spain is a car-free zone. This stretch is easily the most photogenic part of the city, especially when the afternoon light hits the pastel buildings lining the banks. Take a minute here and enjoy the locals leaning against the stone ballastrades to watch the wooden boats drift by. This area stays high energy and always moving, soak in the atmosphere before you head somewhere a bit quieter. Late afternoon, Congress Square. Take a short walk over to Congress Square, one of the most open and grand spaces in the city. After navigating the tighter alleys of the old town, you'll appreciate the breathing room here. The square offers a massive unobstructed view back up toward the castle, making it the perfect spot for photos that capture the fortress looming over the city. Use this as your official pause point. Grab a seat at one of the side street cafes or just find a park bench to reset your legs for a bit. This area has a much more relaxed academic vibe thanks to the massive University of Ljubljana building nearby. It's a practical [music] spot to keep in mind for later as the surrounding streets host some of the best dinner options in town. Take your time here to watch the world go by and plan your final evening moves. Whether you want a quick drink or just a quiet place to sit, the square gives you the space to do it without the riverfront crowds. Early evening cocktails at Nebotnik. From Congress Square, head over to Nebotnik, Ljubljana's historic skyscraper. When it opened in 1933, it was one of the tallest buildings in Europe, and it still feels like a step back into art, decor, glamour. Take the elevator straight to the top floor. You don't need to pay an entrance fee to access the rooftop bar and cafe area. The draw here is the 360° view. You'll look directly across at the castle and over the terracotta rooftops as the city lights start to flicker on. It's the perfect transition spot. One or two drinks, a few panoramic shots, and then you're on your way. Order an apperal spritz or the signature skyscraper spritz made with cocki Americano Rosa and procco. If you're skipping the alcohol, their housemade lemonades are great, too. You should expect to pay between $8 and $14 for a cocktail, which is a total bargain for a view this legendary. Evening dinner near Congress Square. Head back toward Congress Square to wind down. This area is a local favorite for dinner. Trading the riverfront tourist rush for a more sophisticated atmosphere and plenty of open air seating. Grab a table at Costella Chestikica, a city staple serving traditional food since 1776. Order the venison goulash with bread dumplings or the buckwheat shukli rolled dumplings to get a real taste of Slovenia's rustic flavors. For dessert, you have to try the pursa. A massive layered cake with poppy seeds, walnuts, and apples that's legendary in this part of the world. A full dinner with a drink will cost between 28 and $45. It's a relaxed, slowpaced finish to the day that let you eat well and soak in the city lights. Sensure central, it's a short, easy walk back to your bed once you finished. Day two, parks, museums, and city life. Morning. Ljubana Cathedral. Start your second day at the Lubiana Cathedral. Right in the heart of the historic center, this 18th century Baroque masterpiece is the perfect first stop while the city is still waking up and the interior is still at its quietest. The real show is inside. You have to look up. The ceiling's covered in massive vivid fresco that create an incredible sense of depth and scale. Even the doors are a work of art. The massive bronze sloane door and lubian door feature 3D sculpted scenes of the country's history that you can actually feel as you walk in. These were added to mark the Pope's visit in the9s and are easily the most impressive entrance in the city. Entry is a flat $2, and the visit is quick enough that it won't derail your morning. It's a high impact, lowcost stop that sets the tone for the day before you trade the cobblestones for the massive green spaces waiting for you next. Late morning, Tivoli Park. Once you've had your fill of Baroque art, head west toward Tivoli Park, the city's massive emerald backyard. This is where you trade the tight cobblestone alleys for broad treelined paths and wide open lawns. It's the perfect bridge between the historic center and your afternoon plans, giving you the chance to reset and breathe in some fresh air. The highlight of the park is the Jako Peach Prominade, a grand walkway designed by Georgia Pletchnik that leads your eyes straight to the neocclassic Tivoli mansion. This stretch acts as a yearround open air gallery, usually lined with giant high-quality photo exhibits that you can browse for free as you walk. It's an easy flat stroll that feels more like a relaxed garden tour than a standard city park. If you need a quick caffeine hit, stop by Cholana Tivoli, a charming cafe right on the edge of the park's small pond. You can grab a coffee or a local craft beer for around $3 to $5, and just watch the ducks for a minute. The park is completely free to enter and explore, so take your time looping through the wooded trails before you dive back into the city's cultural side. Afternoon Rosnik Hill. Once you finished in Tivoli Park, push deeper into the forest toward Roshnik Hill. This is where the city's green capital title stops being a marketing slogan and starts being a reality. The trails start right behind the park and cut off the city noise almost immediately. There's no pressure to hit a specific summit or follow a strict route. Even 20 minutes of walking get you deep into the woods. This is the local escape used for morning runs and dog walks by people who want to reset without leaving the city limits. Give this 45 minutes to an hour. It provides a massive contrast to the structured museums from earlier in the day and keeps you from hitting that travel burnout wall. You'll head back down feeling refreshed rather than drained, which is exactly the energy you need before the day wraps up. Lunch at Costa Chad. Before you head into the museum, grab a table at Gostel Nachad. This place is a local legend tucked at the base of Roshnik Hill under a canopy of massive trees. It's been run by the same family since 1889, and it's the best spot to break from the city cent's bustle. The menu is famous for grilled specialties. You have to order the chva pachichi which are grilled minced meat rolls or the pleetskavita a spiced bulanstyle burger patties served with a salty clotted cream called kimak. If you want something more local to Slovenia the roasted ve is fantastic. It's honest highquality comfort food that stayed the same for decades. Expect to spend between $18 to $28 for a massive lunch and a cold drink. It's the perfect fuel stop before you spend the afternoon diving into history at the museum just a few minutes away. Afternoon National Museum of Contemporary History. While you're still in Tavi Park, head over to the Second Grod Mansion to find the National Museum of Contemporary History. This is easily the most contenheavy stop of the day, so it's the perfect time to dive in while your energy is still high. The museum walks you through Slovenia's wild 20th century journey from the trenches of World War I through the decades as part of Yugoslavia to the fight for independence in 1991. You aren't just looking at dusty artifacts. The exhibits use immersive setups like a life-sized replica of a World War I trench and a 1950s living room to show you exactly how life shifted under different regimes. It's a fascinating, sometimes heavy look at how this tiny nation carved out its own identity. Entry is about $6 for a standard ticket. It's a small price for a massive amount of context that'll make everything else you see in Ljubana make so much more sense. Insider tip. Don't skip the Slovenians in the 20th century permanent exhibit on the upper floors. It's chronologically laid out so you can literally walk through time. If you're a fan of photography, check out their massive archive of over 2 million negatives. Some of the temporary photo galleries are world class. Evening dinner at Beastro Vicaria. After finishing at the museum, you don't even have to leave the park for a great meal. Just a short walk away is Beastro Shvikaria. A hidden gem set in a beautiful historic alpine style building. It's a favorite for locals who want to escape the city center crowds while enjoying some of the best traditional cooking in Ljubljana. The menu focuses on hearty, honest flavors. You have to try their carneolan sausage or the Idria zeroi, which are small traditional dumplings filled with potato and herbs. If you're looking for something lighter, their seasonal risotos are always a hit. Main dish and drink here will typically [music] cost you between $18 and $30, making it a fantastic value for the quality. It's a quiet scenic spot to wind down your second day. Once you're done, the walk back through the park into the illuminated city center is the perfect way to finish the evening. Useful info for spending 2 days in Ljubljana. Best time to visit. The best window to explore Ljubana is from May through June and again in September and October. The weather's pleasant, ranging from 65 to 75° F or 18 to 24° C. This temperature range is perfect for enjoying the outdoor cafe culture and avoiding the humid peak of midsummer. July and August are the busiest and warmest months. Winter is cold around 30 to 40° F or -1 to 4° C, but offers a festive atmosphere with worldclass holiday markets and fewer crowds. City airport tips. Your arrival point will be Yoja Puchnik Airport located approximately 16 miles or 26 km outside central Lubana. For the most affordable entry, catch the public bus, which takes about 45 minutes and costs roughly $4. If you want a faster connection, the shared shuttle service is your best option at roughly $12. Alternatively, skip the planning with a taxi, which charges a fixed transfer fee of approximately $35 straight to the city center, taking only about 20 minutes to reach your destination. Where to stay in Ljubljana? When choosing your home base in Ljubana, aim for areas that define the city's character. The old town is a fantastic choice, placing you perfectly beneath the castle, near the triple bridge, and along the bustling riverfront. For a completely different energy, focus on Krakovo, famous for its village-like charm, historic gardens, and quiet lanes just minutes from the center. If you're watching your spending, shared hostile beds begin around $30 nightly. For independent rooms, mid-range hotels typically require an investment starting at $120 with rates often climbing toward $250. Where to eat in Ljubana? Ljubljana offers culinary depth across its distinct riverside and historic neighborhoods for a classic historic Slovenian experience. Travel to the base of the castle hill and visit Costila Nagradu. This acclaimed restaurant specializes in authentic dishes from across Slovenia's 24 culinary regions and costs about $30 to $50 per person for a main course. For a buzzing, more casual alternative. Head to the trendy Troubar Evva Street and find Raw Pasta Bar. This lively spot has a modern atmosphere and serves exceptional fresh handmade pasta with meals typically running $15 to $25. Finally, for a place dedicated entirely to plant-based food, look for Gaudian Na. Located near the National Gallery, it's one of the city's premier vegan restaurants, [music] offering creative high-end fusion dishes and usually costing around 20 to $35 per person. Two days in the greenest capital in Europe have officially come to an end. From the dragon guarded bridges of the old town to the forest trails of the Tivoli Park and the local flavors of Gostel Nachad, we've covered it all. If this guide helped you navigate Slovenia, hit like and subscribe for more. Ready for the next chapter? Click here to join us in Croatia, where we're trading the river banks for the stunning coastlines and the ancient stone cities of the Adriatic. >> [music]

https://www.retirementtravelers.com 

Slovenia is one of our favorite countries we have ever visited during our retirement travel. Ljubljana, Slovenia checks all the boxes for a city with beauty, cost of living, safety, great summer weather, castles, bridges, other things to see and do, etc. We enjoyed the Farmer’s market, Open Kitchen, walking around the Old Town and enjoying the bridges, and riding the funicular up to Ljubljana Castle.

We also took a day trip to Lake Bled and it is simply one of the most beautiful places on earth. After hiking up to Bled Castle, we made the 4 mile trek around the lake. We stopped and rented a row boat out to the island and visited the historic church. There were so many picture opportunities, that we probably stopped more than we walked, but it is a day we will never forget.
